network.overall_strategy: This network models glycine as a highly convergent prebiotic target produced through ten literature-grounded routes centered on shared hub molecules HCN, formaldehyde, aminoacetonitrile, glyoxylate, and ammonia. Hydrothermal vent chemistry supplies reduced C1-C3 feedstocks and mineral-mediated glyoxylate/glycine formation; surface environments generate and concentrate HCN, formaldehyde, nitriles, and HCN polymers through spark, UV, cyanosulfidic, eutectic, and Strecker chemistry; biochemical/prebiotic settings complete slower hydrolysis and assembly steps. The network emphasizes the experimentally supported Strecker core, the Bucherer-Bergs branch, HCN polymer hydrolysis, cyanosulfidic production of aminoacetonitrile, hydrothermal glyoxylate-to-glycine synthesis on ferroan brucite, UV-driven reversible amination of glyoxylate, and atmospheric/FTT routes that feed the same intermediates.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline pH 9-11; serpentinization-derived H2; formate reported up to 200 mM

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide catalysts generates reduced C1 species, with formate as the dominant product.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite and magnetite catalyze CO2 + H2 fixation to formate under alkaline hydrothermal conditions around 100C; Beyazay et al. (2023) further supports mineral CO2 reduction in serpentinizing systems.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduced C1 chemistry to formaldehyde

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal reduction; represented as a reduced C1 transfer step connecting vent products to surface Strecker feedstock

reactions.mechanism: Further reduction of C1 carbon on mineral surfaces yields carbonyl equivalents that can be exported as formaldehyde or formaldehyde precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported hydrothermal generation of reduced C1 products including methanol from CO2/H2 on vent minerals, supporting reduced-carbon delivery into downstream surface chemistry. This formaldehyde-forming connection is more speculative than direct formate production but is used as a minimal bridge consistent with the atlas emphasis on formaldehyde as a master precursor.

reactions.name: Atmospheric and surface energy-driven HCN generation

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral assisted photochemical/spark environment
conditions.other: UV or electrical discharge in reducing or CO-containing atmosphere; subsequent dissolution into ponds

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical/electrical processing of reduced carbon-nitrogen feedstocks generates HCN, which is trapped in surface waters and ice matrices.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1955, 1957), Schlesinger & Miller (1983), Johnson et al. (2008), and Parker et al. (2011) establish atmospheric discharge production of HCN feeding Strecker chemistry. TiO2 is included as the required surface mineral agent and is consistent with mineral-assisted photochemistry discussed by Senanayake & Idriss (2006).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical ammonia production and concentration

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow waters; phosphate-buffered surface pools

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical mineral-mediated nitrogen reduction supplies ammonia to nitrile and imine chemistry in surface reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: The pathway atlas identifies atmosphere and mineral surfaces as plausible NH3 sources and specifically notes TiO2 photoreduction of nitrogen as environmentally relevant. Senanayake & Idriss (2006) supports TiO2 photochemistry; this node supplies a literature-consistent NH3 feed for Strecker and related surface chemistry.

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker imine formation from formaldehyde and ammonia

conditions.other: pH near 8; warm pond or aqueous reservoir

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia adds to formaldehyde to form aminomethanol, which dehydrates to methanimine.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1957) and Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) identify this as the first step of the Strecker pathway to glycine; Taillades et al. (1998) used prebiotic aqueous conditions near pH 8 and room temperature for related aminonitrile chemistry.

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker cyanide addition to methanimine

conditions.other: glycinonitrile formation barrier about 15 kcal/mol in water

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide addition to the imine gives aminoacetonitrile, the direct nitrile precursor of glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) explicitly mapped aminoacetonitrile formation from the methanimine intermediate in water and identified it as a kinetically accessible step; Miller (1957) established Strecker chemistry as the core route.

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis to glycinamide

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis; nitrile hydration barrier about 23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Slow aqueous nitrile hydration converts aminoacetonitrile into the corresponding amide, glycinamide.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) identified glycinamide as the explicit hydrolysis intermediate and quantified the barrier for nitrile hydration.

reactions.name: Glycinamide hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: amide hydrolysis is rate-limiting with barrier about 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Aqueous amide hydrolysis yields glycine as the terminal product of the canonical Strecker sequence.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) showed that glycinamide to glycine is the highest-barrier step in the Strecker pathway yet the overall pathway is strongly exergonic.

reactions.name: Glycolonitrile formation from formaldehyde and HCN

conditions.other: Taillades conditions: HCN and HCHO around 1 g/L, pH 8, low NH3

reactions.mechanism: Cyanohydrin formation competes strongly with aminonitrile formation, producing glycolonitrile as a dominant reservoir species.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) reported that under realistic prebiotic conditions glycolonitrile forms at 99.9% while aminoacetonitrile is minor, making this a necessary competing branch in any realistic glycine network.

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile capture by CO2 to hydantoin (Bucherer-Bergs branch)

conditions.other: CO2/bicarbonate-rich solution; hydantoin-forming branch productive at room temperature

reactions.mechanism: CO2 traps aminoacetonitrile into a cyclic ureide reservoir, hydantoin, stabilizing the minor aminonitrile fraction.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) showed CO2 catalyzes conversion of aminoacetonitrile to hydantoin under prebiotic conditions; Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated high-yield hydantoin formation in bicarbonate buffer, confirming the geochemical plausibility of this branch.

reactions.name: Hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolytic opening of the ureide ring

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening of hydantoin generates the carbamoyl-protected amino acid intermediate N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) explicitly identified hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine as the Bucherer-Bergs route toward glycine.

reactions.name: N-carbamoylglycine hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: decarbamoylating hydrolysis in concentrated prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis removes the carbamoyl group to yield free glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) described N-carbamoylglycine as the immediate precursor to glycine in the Bucherer-Bergs branch.

reactions.name: HCN eutectic oligomerization to HCN oligomer pool

conditions.other: productive only above about 0.01-0.1 M HCN; eutectic freezing near -23.4C concentrates HCN to 74.5 mol%

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN undergoes base-catalyzed oligomerization through dimers, trimers, tetramers, and heterogeneous polymeric nitrile-rich solids.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967) established the concentration threshold and eutectic logic for HCN polymerization; Levy et al. (2000) and Menor-Salvan & Marin-Yaseli (2012) support long-term frozen prebiotic HCN chemistry. A fixed single formula is intentionally avoided because the polymer is a heterogeneous oligomer pool.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer pool to glycine

conditions.other: acidic or basic hydrolysis of previously formed HCN polymers; classic ammoniacal HCN aging

reactions.mechanism: Complex nitrile-rich HCN oligomers hydrolyze and rearrange to amino acid mixtures, with glycine generally dominant.
reactions.reasoning: Oró's early ammoniacal HCN work and Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) showed hydrolysis of HCN polymers yields glycine as the most abundant amino acid across many conditions; Matthews & Moser (1966) discussed related polymer-to-amino-acid hypotheses.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline vent chemistry; pyruvate reported at low micromolar yield

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-catalyzed reduction and C-C bond-forming fixation of CO2/H2 produces pyruvate in serpentinizing systems.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from CO2 and H2 on greigite/magnetite-like catalysts at 100C; Beyazay et al. (2023) showed related low-temperature CO2 fixation to pyruvate on Ni3Fe.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal oxidative cleavage connection from pyruvate to glyoxylate

conditions.other: network-connecting keto-acid redistribution step; included conservatively as a speculative bridge

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated keto-acid redox fragmentation provides a route linking vent-generated pyruvate to glyoxylate pools.
reactions.reasoning: The glyoxylate-producing step from pyruvate is not directly established in the supplied glycine literature and is therefore explicitly marked speculative. It is included only to connect the documented pyruvate hub from Preiner et al. (2020) and Beyazay et al. (2023) to the experimentally demonstrated glyoxylate-to-glycine routes.

reactions.name: Ferroan brucite mediated nitrate plus glyoxylate synthesis of glycine

conditions.other: serpentinite-associated, pH 8-9; up to 97% glycine retained on mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reduction of nitrate coupled to amination of glyoxylate yields glycine and simultaneously sequesters product on the mineral surface.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) showed ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate with strong mineral partitioning, making this one of the most direct hydrothermal routes in the supplied literature.

reactions.name: UV-driven reversible amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: UV illumination; modeled on photocatalytic amination of alpha-keto acids

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical reductive amination converts glyoxylate to glycine under mineral-assisted UV conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. (2012) demonstrated reversible amination of alpha-keto acids including glyoxylate to glycine under UV using ZnS. ZnS is not in the permitted agent list, so the reaction is represented within the broader UV-photocatalytic surface mineral context while retaining the literature-grounded chemistry.

reactions.name: Formamide generation from HCN hydrolysis under UV surface conditions

conditions.other: UV-exposed shallow water or mineral film

reactions.mechanism: HCN hydration/hydrolysis in irradiated aqueous environments yields formamide as a persistent reactive intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied review identifies HCN + H2O + UV to formamide as a relevant surface process, and Senanayake & Idriss (2006) showed TiO2-mediated photochemistry of formamide to biomolecular products.

reactions.name: Formamide-derived glycine formation under TiO2 photocatalysis

conditions.other: UV-irradiated mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Photocatalytic activation of formamide on mineral surfaces produces amino acid products including glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Senanayake & Idriss (2006) reported TiO2-mediated conversion of formamide under UV to biomolecular products including amino acids; the glycine-specific route is less mechanistically resolved than Strecker chemistry but is literature-grounded as a surface photochemical branch.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic production of aminoacetonitrile from HCN feedstock

conditions.other: H2S and UV present; phosphate-buffered solution around 35C

reactions.mechanism: A unified cyanosulfidic network reductively homologates HCN and nitrogen feedstocks to aminonitriles including aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate buffer at 35C produces aminoacetonitrile as part of a unified systems chemistry network generating amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid precursors.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type catalyst generation of HCN from reduced gases

conditions.catalyst: metallic iron and iron oxide surfaces
conditions.other: CO-rich, reducing catalytic zone analogous to meteoritic/native metal FTT chemistry

reactions.mechanism: High-temperature heterogeneous catalysis generates simple reduced organics including cyanide that can be transported into cooler aqueous zones.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) showed amino acid-forming FTT chemistry on meteoritic iron catalysts, with HCN and formaldehyde generated on hot metal surfaces and Strecker chemistry occurring in cooler zones; Schlesinger & Miller (1983) supports the relevance of CO-rich atmospheres.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type catalyst generation of formaldehyde from reduced gases

conditions.other: high-temperature catalytic zone with cooling gradient to aqueous trap

reactions.mechanism: FTT-type reduction of carbon oxides on iron-bearing surfaces generates aldehydic carbonyl species including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) identified formaldehyde generation on catalytic metal surfaces as part of amino-acid-producing FTT systems; this route feeds the same Strecker hub as atmospheric and hydrothermal C1 chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate reductive amination to methylamine and small amines pool

conditions.other: anoxic mineral-surface amination/decarboxylation side chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Keto-acid/nitrogen chemistry on iron-sulfur surfaces produces simple amines that can participate in downstream glycine-related chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Altwegg et al. (2016) detected methylamine as a glycine precursor in cometary material, and the supplied review identifies amines as relevant precursor reservoirs. This step is a connecting, comparatively speculative hydrothermal amine branch included to keep pyruvate linked into the wider glycine precursor network.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and ammonia form methanimine, cyanide addition yields aminoacetonitrile, and stepwise hydrolysis gives glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs branch from aminoacetonitrile
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2 as hydantoin, then hydrolyzed through N-carbamoylglycine to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated eutectic HCN oligomerizes to a heterogeneous polymer pool that hydrolyzes to amino acids with glycine dominant.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Unified HCN/H2S/UV systems chemistry produces aminoacetonitrile directly, which hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal ferroan brucite glyoxylate route
pathways.description: Serpentinization-derived glyoxylate and nitrate yield glycine directly on ferroan brucite with strong mineral sequestration.

pathways.name: UV-driven glyoxylate amination route
pathways.description: Surface photochemical reductive amination of glyoxylate by ammonia gives glycine directly.

pathways.name: Formamide photochemistry route
pathways.description: HCN hydrolyzes to formamide under UV surface conditions and formamide photochemistry yields glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal C1 to Strecker route
pathways.description: Vent CO2 reduction produces formate and formaldehyde-equivalent carbon that is transferred to the surface Strecker network.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch formaldehyde-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: High-temperature iron-catalyzed FTT chemistry generates formaldehyde that feeds the canonical Strecker route to glycine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch HCN-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: High-temperature iron-catalyzed FTT chemistry generates HCN that converges with surface formaldehyde and ammonia into the Strecker sequence.
